abstract: While Thomas Jefferson famously asserted that all men are created equal, the historical treatment of migrants tells a far more complicated story. In this volume, twelve distinguished scholars analyze how nations—including France, the United States, Turkey, Canada, Mexico, the Netherlands, Sweden, and Denmark—have historically categorized and differentiated among migrants, as well as the rhetoric used to justify such distinctions in policy and public discourse. The chapters are organized into a triptych, exploring three central questions: who qualifies as a refugee, who constitutes a family, and how "difference" is defined. Collectively, these studies demonstrate that these categories are inextricably linked. They intersect in complex ways, shaped not only by geopolitics and economic shifts but also by the specific gender, class, ethnicity, religion, and sexual orientation of the migrants themselves.
